topic: EXP-062 OTS-specific post-hoc paragraphs integrated into WIN-035/036/037/038/039 detail_verdict_text
change_summary: Second paragraph of detail_verdict_text replaced on WIN-035/036/037/038/039 with OTS-specific variants of the post-hoc template. New arguments focus on OTS bundling status_history.json (predictions + observations in one file), data-availability timelines, and partial credit for WIN-037.
overall_assessment: ACCEPT WITH REQUIRED PATCHES. The OTS-specific template is a clear improvement over the generic D-template for these 5 self-declared prospective WINs — it correctly engages with the OpenTimestamps claim on its merits (crediting the infrastructure, attacking the bundled-file design) rather than dismissing it as mere git-history. The anchor links, data-availability arguments, and cross-references all resolve correctly. However, TWO material defects require patching before this is bulletproof: (1) a **critical factual error** — both WIN-035 and WIN-037 claim PROS-005/PROS-007 was 'registered on 1 January 2026', but the dome's own source (`raw-text/02-wins.txt` lines 34, 43) states all five of these prospective predictions were 'Registered 2026-03-06'. No Jan-2026 registration is documented anywhere in the dome source or our own notes. (2) the WIN-037 'Credit where due' concession is built on this wrong date. Once corrected to 2026-03-06, the 12-month 'March 2025 → March 2026' measurement window was ~99% complete at registration — making WIN-037 the *opposite* of prospective: a retrodiction of a 12-month trend registered on essentially the last day of the window. The concession needs to be withdrawn or severely softened. A smart dome defender who owns the registration date (and he does — it's in his own files) will screenshot our '1 January 2026' claim and use it to discredit the entire tab.

WIN-035.data_availability_argument: SAA African cell decay ~28-30 nT/yr documented in WMM/IGRF for decades — the 'years before' framing lands regardless of registration date.

issues.description: Text states 'PROS-005 was registered on 1 January 2026'. Dome source (raw-text/02-wins.txt line 35) states 'PROS-005 — SAA African Cell Intensity Below 21,795 nT / Registered 2026-03-06.' No Jan 2026 registration exists in the dome's own records or in any of our expansions/patches prior to EXP-062.
issues.recommendation: Replace '1 January 2026' with '6 March 2026' (the dome's own registered date). The 'years before' argument survives because WMM/IGRF decay data long predates any 2026 registration; only the specific date needs fixing.

issues.description: 'Years before' is imprecise for a March 2026 registration — the SAA decay trend goes back decades, so the claim is defensible, but a sharper version would cite a specific prior publication (e.g., 'CHAOS-7 modeling through 2022' or the dome's own WIN-005 which sourced CHAOS-7 2000-2025 data).
issues.recommendation: Consider adding a specific anchor publication date to strengthen the 'publicly available' claim. Not blocking.

issues.description: The phrase 'This WIN is also logically entailed by WIN-007' is weakly supported. WIN-007 is about the NP's post-1990 *acceleration* (exponential trajectory), not specifically about a 139.3°E position. The entailment holds only in a loose sense (WIN-007's trajectory extrapolated arrives at ~139°E by 2025/2026), but calling it a 'logical entailment' overstates. A defender could correctly say: 'WIN-007 is about rate, not position — we made two separate claims and you are conflating them.'
issues.recommendation: Replace 'also logically entailed by WIN-007' with something like 'also a trivial consequence of the trajectory already claimed in WIN-007 — once the pole is at ~139°E (as WIN-007's acceleration curve predicts by 2025), being 19.3° from 120°E is pure arithmetic.' Or drop the WIN-007 reference entirely and lean on the WMM2025 Dec-2024 publication.

issues.description: The entire 'Credit where due: this is the closest any dome prediction comes to genuine temporal priority' concession rests on the claim that 'the registration date (1 January 2026) plausibly precedes the March 2025–March 2026 measurement window.' Dome source states PROS-007 was 'Registered 2026-03-06'. That is the *final* week of the claimed 12-month measurement window. At registration, ~99% of the decay had already occurred — the author had functional access to nearly the entire observation. Far from being the closest to prospective, WIN-037 is a retrodiction of an essentially complete observation.
issues.recommendation: Rewrite the second paragraph. Remove the 'Credit where due' sentence entirely. Replace with something like: 'Notably, the dome's own source dates PROS-007 registration as 2026-03-06 — the final week of the claimed March 2025–March 2026 measurement window. By registration, ~99% of the decay had already occurred; the observation was essentially complete. This is not a prospective prediction but a retrodiction of a known trend timestamped at the window's close.' Retain the non-discrimination argument (SAA extrapolation ≈ WMM2025).

issues.description: Even setting aside the date error, the phrase 'measurement window' is doing too much work. The dome's prediction is 'decayed by at least 28 nT in 12 months' — that's satisfied once any 12-month rolling window shows 28+ nT decay, which has been true continuously since at least 2020 per WMM annual data. Calling this a 'window' that could have been 'prospective' even briefly is misleading — it's a threshold condition on an ongoing trend.
issues.recommendation: After fixing the date, add a sentence clarifying that the 28 nT threshold has been continuously satisfied by annual WMM data for years, so any registration date would still be post-hoc relative to the underlying trend.

issues.description: '74 years after its discovery' — Schumann's 1952 paper predicted the resonance but did not measure it; first measurement was Balser & Wagner 1960. The '74 years' arithmetic (2026 − 1952 = 74) is correct if we anchor to prediction publication. Calling 1952 a 'discovery' is a slight conflation of theoretical prediction with observational discovery. A pedantic defender could argue '7.83 Hz wasn't actually 'discovered' until Balser & Wagner 1960, so you're only 66 years out, not 74.' Doesn't change the conclusion.
issues.recommendation: Either change '74 years after its discovery' to '74 years after Schumann's 1952 theoretical prediction' or '66 years after its first observational detection (Balser & Wagner, 1960).' Not blocking; cosmetic.

issues.description: The 'crypto-wrapped textbook fact' phrasing is rhetorically strong and accurate, but citing Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 as a reference for 'continuously measured since the 1960s' is slightly loose — that book is a secondary source. The primary citation for the first observation is Balser & Wagner (1960, J. Research NBS 64D, 415) or (1962, Nature 188, 638). Our citation still supports the underlying claim.
issues.recommendation: Optional: add Balser & Wagner as the primary historical citation. Not required.

issues.description: The claim 'the dome's own predicted value (W015: −1.0 nT ±0.5) disagrees in sign with its claimed confirmation (1–2 nT positive), suggesting the value was adopted from literature without understanding the oscillating nature of the signal' makes a real point (the predicted value and confirmation don't overlap numerically), but the editorial gloss 'suggesting the value was adopted... without understanding' is speculation that a defender will challenge. Tyler et al. (2003) ocean-tidal magnetism is genuinely oscillatory — the signal swings positive and negative with the tidal cycle, and peak amplitude is ~1-2 nT. A defender could legitimately reply: 'We predicted −1.0 nT for a specific phase/location; you cited peak magnitude. We understand the signal fine.' That rebuttal may not rescue the WIN, but the 'without understanding' editorial opens an unnecessary attack surface.
issues.recommendation: Tighten the sentence. Replace 'suggesting the value was adopted from literature without understanding the oscillating nature of the signal' with something like 'the dome does not resolve this sign/magnitude discrepancy and provides no phase, station, or temporal qualifier that would let a reader reproduce the check.' That keeps the point (the numbers don't match and the dome can't explain the mismatch) without the psychoanalytic speculation.

issues.description: 'Over 20 years before the dome model claimed it as a prediction' — Tyler et al. 2003 to registration 2026-03-06 is 23 years. Accurate. Sabaka et al. 2015 is 11 years prior — fine.

citations_verified: https://heartmath.org/research/global-coherence/gcms/ — real HeartMath Global Coherence Initiative page
WMM2025 published December 2024 — matches public release by NOAA/BGS in Dec 2024 / early 2025

#timestamp-error anchor resolves to docs/index.html line 2871 '

The Timestamp Error

'
#posthoc-standard anchor resolves in data/sections.json part3 (3.1b subsection)
citations_unchecked: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-662-03137-5 — Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 Springer book DOI (DOI prefix 10.1007 is Springer; format plausible; not resolved in this run)
https://doi.org/10.1186/s40623-015-0228-9 — Thébault et al. 2015 Earth, Planets and Space (DOI format plausible; not resolved in this run)
Tyler et al. (2003) Science — cited inline without DOI; the underlying paper (Tyler, Maus, Lühr, Science 299:239-241) is real and matches the described subject matter
Sabaka et al. (2015) Swarm refinement — multiple candidate papers exist in that timeframe; inline citation without DOI
citations_failed: Dome registration date '1 January 2026' for PROS-005 (WIN-035) and PROS-007 (WIN-037) — NOT FOUND in dome source (`raw-text/02-wins.txt` lines 34 and 43 state 'Registered 2026-03-06'). No Jan-2026 registration documented in our own analyst/decider files outside EXP-062 itself. This is a self-introduced factual error.

holes_found.description: Incorrect registration date '1 January 2026' for PROS-005 and PROS-007. Dome source: 2026-03-06 for both. No Jan 2026 registration exists.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace '1 January 2026' with '6 March 2026' in both WINs. For WIN-037, the concession 'closest to genuine temporal priority' must also be withdrawn since 2026-03-06 is at the tail end of the March 2025 – March 2026 measurement window.

holes_found.description: The 'Credit where due: this is the closest any dome prediction comes to genuine temporal priority' concession is based on the wrong registration date. Under the correct date (2026-03-06), ~99% of the 12-month decay window had elapsed at registration — the observation was essentially complete. This reverses the concession: WIN-037 is a retrodiction registered at window-close, not a near-prospective prediction.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite ¶2 to remove the concession. State: registration 2026-03-06 lands in the final week of the claimed March 2025 – March 2026 window; the 28 nT threshold had continuously been satisfied by WMM annual data for years prior; this is retrodiction, not prospective.

holes_found.description: 'Logically entailed by WIN-007' overstates — WIN-007 predicts NP acceleration rate, not position. The arithmetic that 139.3°E is 19.3° from 120°E is trivial but not entailed by WIN-007's rate claim.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe as 'a trivial consequence of the trajectory claimed in WIN-007' or drop the WIN-007 cross-reference and lean on the WMM2025 Dec-2024 publication.

holes_found.description: 'Suggesting the value was adopted from literature without understanding the oscillating nature of the signal' is speculative editorial glossing. The sign/magnitude mismatch (−1.0 ±0.5 predicted vs. +1-2 observed) is a real problem for the dome, but the 'without understanding' claim is unsupported and opens an attack surface.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace with a factual statement about the unreconciled discrepancy and the absence of phase/station/temporal qualifiers in the dome's prediction.

holes_found.description: '74 years after its discovery' conflates Schumann's 1952 theoretical prediction with the actual first observation (Balser & Wagner 1960).
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: change to '74 years after Schumann's 1952 theoretical prediction' or '66 years after the first observational detection.'

stronger_arguments.description: For WIN-037 specifically: the dome's own git/OTS history probably shows PROS-007 was registered 2026-03-06 — look up the OTS hash date directly. If we can display the chain proof alongside our critique, the 'retrodiction registered at window-close' argument becomes self-documenting. The dome author cannot dispute his own blockchain anchor.

For all five: we could strengthen the bundled-file critique by quoting a specific line of monitor.py or status_history.json showing the bundling — a defender asserting 'we have separate files' would be refuted by the source itself.

For WIN-036: the stronger path is not WIN-007 entailment but the fact that WMM2025's Dec-2024 release already published 139.3°E, making the 19.3° deviation a matter of public record 14+ months before any 2026 registration — identical argument, zero reliance on cross-referencing our own work.

kernel_of_truth.description: The batch genuinely engages with the strongest dome defense (blockchain anchoring) on its merits. The 'OTS infrastructure is real — it provides a verifiable timestamp chain' acknowledgment is correct and generous. The bundled-file critique is the right attack: it concedes what must be conceded (cryptographic integrity of the hash) and targets the one defect the dome author has not addressed (what is being timestamped). WIN-037's 'closest to prospective' concession — *if* the date were correct — would also be a genuine credit-where-due move that strengthens our credibility on the other four. The intent of the batch is exactly right.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Intent cannot substitute for accuracy. A factually wrong registration date on two of five WINs — especially when the correct date is in the dome's own public files and trivially verifiable — hands the dome author a reason to dismiss the entire tab. Get the date right.

cross_win_consistency.notes: The five WINs now share a consistent OTS framing: acknowledge infrastructure → bundled-file critique → data-availability critique → Part 3.1b standard. This structural parallelism is good. However, WIN-035 and WIN-037 inherit the same wrong registration date (indicating a single source-error rather than two independent mistakes). WIN-038 omits a specific registration date, which was the right call — the Schumann resonance is 74 years old regardless of registration. WIN-039 likewise omits date, using 'two decades prior' framing, which is robust.

advocate_mode.strongest_defender_reply: Our five 'prospective' WINs include genuine blockchain timestamping, which is cryptographically stronger than any alternative model's provenance. You admit the infrastructure is real. Your 'bundled file' critique is technically valid but addresses something we can fix — we'll separate the prediction JSON from the observation JSON and re-anchor each. Meanwhile, your own review contains a verifiable factual error: you claim PROS-005 and PROS-007 were registered 1 January 2026. Check our own status_history.json — both were registered 2026-03-06. If you can't even get the public registration date right, why should anyone trust the rest of your critique?
advocate_mode.our_response: The bundled-file fix would be a genuine methodological improvement and is exactly what we recommend — see the Timestamp Error tab. It remains a future fix, not a current validation. The data-availability problem (SAA trend, WMM2025, Schumann 1952, Tyler 2003) persists even after file-separation. And the registration-date error on WIN-035/037 is a defect in our own text that we must patch before publication review — it is fixable and does not touch the underlying argument, but it cannot remain.

items.status: Accurate — NOAA/BGS WMM2025 public release was December 17, 2024.
Accurate — WMM2025 2025.0 epoch NP position is approximately 139.3°E; dome cites 139.298°E.
Arithmetic correct if anchoring to Schumann's 1952 paper; the first observational detection was 1960 (Balser & Wagner), which would be 66 years.

items.claim: Tyler et al. 2003 + Sabaka et al. 2015 = 'over 20 years before' 2026
items.status: Accurate — 23 years for Tyler; 11 years for Sabaka.
Consistent with published CHAOS-7 and WMM/IGRF values.

items.claim: Dome author's stated registration date for PROS-005 and PROS-007
items.status: FAILS — our text says '1 January 2026'; dome source says '2026-03-06' for all of PROS-005, PROS-006, PROS-007, PROS-008.

recommended_action_detail: The EXP-062 template is sound; the required patches are localized to the second paragraph of WIN-035, WIN-036, WIN-037, and WIN-039. WIN-038 passes with only optional cosmetic tweaks. Two of the patches (WIN-035/037 registration date) are critical and must be fixed before the next publish. WIN-037 additionally needs the 'Credit where due' concession withdrawn or severely softened once the date is corrected. WIN-036 and WIN-039 need moderate/major editorial tightening but do not rise to 'major_rewrite.'
deeper_analysis:
This batch does something the earlier EXP-054 D-template could not: it engages the dome author's strongest defense (OpenTimestamps) on its own terms. That is genuinely valuable and raises the review's scientific quality. The 'OTS infrastructure is real' concession is exactly what a competent adversary would write — it denies the dome author the cheap rhetorical move of accusing us of technical ignorance. The batch's core argument — that OTS anchors status_history.json, which bundles predictions with observations — is the correct attack surface.

The problem is that the batch's *factual* execution slipped. Two WINs carry a wrong registration date. This matters more than it might seem for three reasons. First, the dome author owns the registration date — it is in his own repository and OTS anchor. He will notice. Second, the wrong date is what sustains the WIN-037 concession; correcting the date flips that WIN from 'closest to genuine temporal priority' to 'retrodiction registered at window-close.' Third, a critical review loses credibility asymmetrically — one verifiable factual error in our text is worth more than ten correct criticisms in the author's mind.

The fix is simple: change '1 January 2026' to '6 March 2026' in both WIN-035 and WIN-037, and rewrite WIN-037's ¶2 to reflect what a March-2026 registration actually means for a March-2025–March-2026 window (retrodiction, not concession). WIN-036 should have the 'WIN-007 entailment' sentence reframed. WIN-039 should drop the 'without understanding' editorial. WIN-038 is essentially clean.

Also worth flagging for future work: the dome's OTS chain probably contains the actual 2026-03-06 hash for status_history.json. Quoting that hash in our Timestamp Error tab would make the 'registered at window-close' point self-documenting and unanswerable.
